full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Over the last two Sundays, a man named Tracy walked into our church doors in need of help.<|7.36|> <|8.36|> The name can mean three or brave warrior.<|12.00|> <|13.28|> A member of our staff informed me of his arrival.<|16.56|> <|17.76|> Deacon Jack, there's a man in the welcome center needing assistance.<|22.00|> <|22.00|> Deacon Jack, there's a man in the welcome center needing assistance.<|22.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As I'm in conversation with a parishioner during coffee hour, another person comes over<|6.32|> <|6.32|> and interrupts with some urgency in their voice to let me know, "Deacon Jack, there's<|12.34|> <|12.34|> a man in the Welcome Center needing assistance."<|15.12|> <|15.12|> I respond saying, "Yes, I know.<|17.68|> <|17.68|> It's okay.<|18.68|> <|18.68|> I'll be there in a few minutes.<|20.02|> <|20.02|> They can wait."<|21.96|> <|21.96|> And I honor my word and arrive soon after to greet my brother Tracy.<|27.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As I sat with him and heard bits of his story, I began to understand he was unhoused and suffering from mental illness. He spoke with a stutter, yet was so gentle, kind, and loving that he was so gentle and loving.<|16.30|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> He told me he was hungry and needed a bus ticket to Salt Lake City.<|5.60|> <|5.60|> But I had already given out all the community discretionary funds for the week, so I got<|11.06|> <|11.06|> him some food, gave him one of our little blue resource packets, and said to return next<|18.66|> <|18.66|> Sunday.<|19.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Before saying goodbye, I asked him if he would join me for a cup of coffee and a cookie outside.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> I do this, of course, so that our neighbors feel welcomed in this place, and so that all<|16.06|> <|16.06|> of you can witness the humbling, beautiful richness of welcoming Christ in our midst,<|22.78|> <|23.42|> especially where we might least expect it, in the poor.<|27.44|> <|27.44|> Amen.<|28.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Lo and behold, after the words of dismissal last Sunday, "Let us go forth in the name<|8.32|> <|8.32|> of Christ," who shows up but Tracy.<|13.78|> <|13.78|> It was such a powerful moment of how our worship and life as Christians dramatically connect.<|22.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In a wonderful book called "Praying Shapes Believing," the author, Leonel Mitchell,<|7.94|> <|7.94|> writes, and a quick aside here, "Right when I was opening my book to copy down this quote<|14.32|> <|14.32|> into my sermon, I accidentally pressed a mysterious combination of keys on my keyboard, which opened<|20.40|> <|20.40|> up a little emoji window, and the winking emoji was coincidentally pressed and inserted into<|26.50|> <|26.50|> my sermon."<|29.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But that's perhaps beside the point, so here is the quote.<|4.98|> <|6.54|> Dismissal, Missa in Latin, has given its name to the entire service, the Mass.<|13.86|> <|14.82|> The Church which has gathered to make Eucharist is sent out to be the body of Christ in the world,<|22.48|> <|22.90|> to bear witness to Jesus and the resurrection.<|26.18|> <|26.18|> Amen.<|27.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The dismissal asserts the relationship between liturgy and Christian living.<|5.84|> <|6.84|> It is a call to mission, a call to live the baptismal and Eucharistic life in the world.<|14.22|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> - Wow.<|1.78|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Now, maybe you have, but I don't know if I've ever had the words of dismissal call me into action so quickly after walking out of our church doors.<|10.84|> <|11.68|> But there was the need. There was my neighbor, my brother Tracy, in search of love, in search of healing.<|18.86|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> -<|1.04|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The infinite question our faith challenges us to consider each day is that of who is my neighbor?<|9.52|> <|9.52|> Is it those who just look like me, share a common identity or faith, political party, gender, or sexual orientation?<|20.30|> <|20.30|> Is it those who just look like me, share a common identity or faith, political party, gender, or sexual orientation?<|21.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Or is it more? Is my neighbor, my fellow human being, all of that and all that appears to be different?<|9.88|> <|11.38|> That is the challenge posed by James in this morning's epistle about drawing distinctions between rich and poor<|20.16|> <|20.16|> and by the Gentile woman of Syrophoenician descent begging Jesus to heal her sick daughter.<|28.18|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Our gospel continues with a slight tweaking.<|5.24|> <|5.24|> Then looking up to heaven, he sighed and said to him,<|10.08|> <|10.54|> Ephatha, Ephatha, that is be opened.<|15.60|> <|16.84|> And his ears were open.<|18.76|> <|18.92|> Her tongue was released and they spoke plainly.<|24.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This dramatic moment when Jesus heals a deaf man with a speech impediment reminds me of my encounters with Tracy, our worship here and ministry in the world.<|14.72|> <|16.20|> And no, it's probably not about putting our fingers in our ears, but I do believe it is about being open, being open.<|25.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Being open to new ideas and new possibilities.<|4.68|> <|5.58|> Being open to trusting in God and being open to caring for each other.<|12.26|> <|14.24|> So where might God invite you to be open in this new season?<|20.28|> <|20.28|> Amen.<|21.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Is it in your prayers, your relationships or service, one of our many small groups or community partners, a call to ordained ministry, or a phone call to an estranged relative or old friend?<|20.20|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> For what might happen when we are open to the love of God and the love of our neighbor?<|8.36|> <|9.62|> What might we hear or see but the glory of the kingdom of heaven?<|16.44|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and<|1.56|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.20|> There is a fitting prayer in our Book of Common Prayer<|4.40|> <|4.40|> that I would like to close with today,<|6.96|> <|7.84|> a prayer for the social order,<|10.04|> <|11.04|> a prayer for social justice.<|13.42|> <|14.74|> So let us pray.<|15.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.00|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Grant, O God, that your holy and life-giving Spirit may so move and open each human heart<|11.60|> <|11.60|> in this place, in this country, in this world, that barriers which divide us may crumble,<|19.86|> <|20.90|> suspicions disappear, and hatreds cease.<|23.94|> <|23.94|> Amen.<|24.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> that our divisions being healed,<|2.86|> <|3.44|> we may live in justice and peace<|5.76|> <|5.76|> through Jesus Christ our Lord.<|8.76|> <|9.80|> Amen.<|10.26|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|2.24|><|endoftext|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Now, maybe you have, but I don't know if I've ever had the words of dismissal call me into action so quickly after walking out of our church doors.<|10.84|>
<|11.68|> But there was the need. There was my neighbor, my brother Tracy, in search of love, in search of healing.<|18.86|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The infinite question our faith challenges us to consider each day is that of who is my neighbor?<|9.52|>
<|9.52|> Is it those who just look like me, share a common identity or faith, political party, gender, or sexual orientation?<|20.30|>
<|20.30|> Is it those who just look like me, share a common identity or faith, political party, gender, or sexual orientation?<|21.30|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This dramatic moment when Jesus heals a deaf man with a speech impediment reminds me of my encounters with Tracy, our worship here and ministry in the world.<|14.72|>
<|16.20|> And no, it's probably not about putting our fingers in our ears, but I do believe it is about being open, being open.<|25.70|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Being open to new ideas and new possibilities.<|4.68|>
<|5.58|> Being open to trusting in God and being open to caring for each other.<|12.26|>
<|14.24|> So where might God invite you to be open in this new season?<|20.28|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Is it in your prayers, your relationships or service, one of our many small groups or community partners, a call to ordained ministry, or a phone call to an estranged relative or old friend?<|20.20|><|endoftext|>
